data.title: Making America's Chip Fabs Great Again
data.description:
By Yan Taw (YT) Boon

As national security threats mount, the U.S. aims to revive production of complex computer chips, creating potential opportunities all along the semi supply chain.

The U.S. used to be the global leader in semiconductor manufacturing. Yet decades of offshoring have left the country heavily reliant on advanced computer chips made in Taiwan, Japan and South Korea. To put the current imbalance in perspective: Taiwan’s TSMC (TSM), the world’s largest chipmaker, now produces more than half of the world’s advanced chips.1

While it’s true that U.S. juggernauts such as Nvidia (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Qualcomm (QCOM) and Broadcom (AVGO) have revolutionized everything from wireless communication to artificial intelligence, and that members of the Magnificent 7 (including Apple, Amazon and Tesla) now design their own chips, none of these companies make the little engines chugging at the core of their products. There would be no Apple iPhones or Tesla cars without Taiwan’s chip manufacturing ecosystem.

Yet two recent developments may be marking a crucial turning point for the U.S. semiconductor industry, potentially unlocking attractive opportunities for selective investors all along the semi supply chain.

In March 2025, TSMC announced a fresh $100 billion investment to build various chip facilities in the U.S. — the largest single foreign direct investment in U.S. history. Specifically, the Taiwanese giant plans to establish five plants, including three fabs, two packaging facilities, and a research and development center.2

Also in March, Intel (INTC), which has lost ground to competitors in recent years, appointed industry veteran Lip-Bu Tan as its new CEO. Renowned for his successful turnaround of chip design software firm Cadence (CDNS) and his impact on the Electronic Design Automation (EDA) industry over the past decade, Tan is expected to prioritize expanding Intel’s foundry services and advancing AI chip production in seeking to regain the firm’s formerly formidable manufacturing prowess.3

We believe these moves — and perhaps more to come — could signal a broader revival across the U.S. semiconductor ecosystem, creating ripple effects across the economy and unlocking compelling opportunities for investors. The expansion of U.S.-based fabs is expected to drive significant growth in sectors critical to chip production, including equipment manufacturing (think complex lithography machines and wafer-handling equipment), construction and specialty chemicals, among other areas.

Both the Biden and Trump administrations have emphasized the strategic importance of semiconductors, framing the revival of domestic chip manufacturing as both a technological imperative and a matter of economic security. According to the Semiconductor Industry Association (SIA), U.S. fab capacity is projected to grow by an impressive 203% between 2022 and 2032.4

For investors, we believe this resurgence arrives at an opportune time, as semiconductor stock valuations have retreated in the wake of DeepSeek’s announcement that it had developed a large language AI model requiring far less computing power (i.e. fancy chips). With demand for critical production inputs set to accelerate, we believe companies at the forefront of a potential U.S. chipmaking renaissance are strategically positioned to capitalize on emerging opportunities.

Footnotes

1 “Taiwan’s dominance of the chip industry makes it more important,” The Economist, March 6, 2023;

2 “Trump and TSMC announce $100 billion plan to build five new U.S. factories”, Reuters, March 4, 2025;

3 “Exclusive: Intel’s new CEO plots overhaul of manufacturing and rel="noopener noreferrer" AI operations,” Reuters, March 17, 2025;

4“Strengthening the U.S. Semiconductor Supply Chain,” SIA, 2024.

data.title: Qualcomm And The Explosion Of Edge Computing
data.description:
Thesis

Qualcomm (NASDAQ:QCOM) is one of the names I’ve followed for the longest time – nearly three decades. Back in the 1990s Investor’s Business Daily (an up & coming newspaper) highlighted the trials & tribulations of the “Holy War of Wireless Standards” to which Qualcomm’s CDMA protocol eventually won. Since then, Qualcomm’s power rose steadily as the U.S. cellular phone penetration-rate rose from the low single digits to 100%. Qualcomm’s potential ebbed as wireless matured. Going forward, I see a new market in IoT (Internet of Things) that will leverage Qualcomm’s core competencies and expand its TAM.

Introduction:

Qualcomm is an important enabler within the wireless industry. It designs semiconductors, software & services focused around wireless technology. The company has one of the largest patent portfolios in the world. It operates under two business segments: QCT (semiconductors) QTL (technology licensing). This article is geared towards QCOM’s potential to capture businesses within exponential technologies and will not focus on recent results.

Semiconductor Industry

The chip industry can best be described as growth-cyclical. There is a constant expansion (13% CAGR) of TAM ($697Bn) due to expanding use-cases. The industry has shifted from being a vertically integrated one to “fabless” where companies outsource production. Companies, thus, have become more specialized (Micron: memory, Nvidia: GPU, Intel: CPU) and some companies (e.g., ARM) focus on IP licensing.

Internet of Things (IoT) market

IoT wirelessly connects all kinds of devices to each other, to you and to the Internet via Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, etc. Honestly, when I first heard of IoT, I considered it yet another “shiny object” the industry was promoting. I once questioned, “Do we REALLY need our toaster to be connected ?!” We’ve come a long way since and the benefits of IoT are growing daily. IoT allows for remote control and smart assistants, so homeowners could control their lights, thermostats and appliances. It helps lower electricity prices by reducing heating and shutting lights when you’re not home.

Outside of the home, wearables can help track vitals and promote healthier living. And the list goes on. What particularly interested me is that Moore’s Law is constantly improving semiconductor chips and services, allowing IoT to grow in importance. Let’s not forget that in 2023, Nvidia (NVDA) had its “iPhone Moment” which transformed traditional computer intelligence into AI and neural networks. If what I’ve just said doesn’t convince you of the merits of IoT, then consider that AI Agents will again transform IoT, allowing for real-time processing, seamless communication and context awareness. For example, an AI Agent can schedule maintenance on an HVAC system based on its usage data.

Total Addressable Market (TAM)

At Qualcomm’s 2024 Investor Day, management gave a series of presentations I encourage you to read. Management estimates the total “Edge-computing” TAM at $900Bn by 2030. While Qualcomm was clearly “talking their book” I’m confident this TAM could be reached given what other companies (Nvidia, Tesla, Microsoft) are doing in the space.

Automotive Market

I’m particularly impressed with Qualcomm’s automotive business, but its revenue scale (< $3.3Bn TTM) doesn’t tell the whole story.

They’ve managed to “land and expand” in what I consider the most lucrative areas: infotainment systems and autonomous. QCOM’s automotive pipeline is nearly $50Bn and if they recognized that revenue, it would easily become its largest end market. Their autonomous auto-stack was co-developed with BMW (OTC:BMWKY) and will commercialize next year, allowing Qualcomm to recognize significant revenues on its financial statements. Qualcomm management’s targeting the Auto segment to grow from $3Bn to $8Bn annually by FY ’29. The “QCT IoT” business plus “Auto” together is targeted at $22Bn in FY ’29, which would be over 50% of Qualcomm’s total FY ’24 revenues!

Beyond 2029

Qualcomm’s XR platform offers the most innovative and exciting prospects. While it’s a small business segment for Qualcomm (just $2Bn FY ’29 targeted), I see great optionality as it leverages QCOM’s core competencies and offers a “blue-ocean” of demand. Readers need to experience (as I have) Virtual Reality, Augmented Reality and Mixed Reality in what feels like a Sci-fi movie.

Revenue Growth and Balance Sheet

I won’t go into details on Qualcomm’s financial ratios as the company has been well stewarded since its IPO. For example, it pays a dividend in a responsible manner and while it has debt, its Net Debt (after cash equivalents) is zero. Qualcomm has had somewhat volatile revenue growth – surprising considering the somewhat predictable global handset market. Its efforts to diversify into automotive and IoT markets should lessen this volatility and lift valuation multiples.

Recent Results & Outlook

Qualcomm’s 1Q ’25 earnings results were good overall as results beat estimates. However, QCOM’s shares declined after-hours on lowered guidance. For additional information, I encourage you to read SA author Daniel Schönberger’s earnings analysis.

Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG)

Qualcomm ranks highly for ESG. New (2018) CEO Cristiano Amon has been instrumental in shaping Qualcomm’s direction towards new initiatives. The CEO appointment boosted its corporate governance as the Jacobs family (which founded the company in 1985) no longer has direct influence. (Note: “G” in the table means Qualcomm hasn’t been involved in any major controversies.)

Risks:

Given that the wireless services industry is an oligopolistic one, Qualcomm benefits from having a few large, creditworthy customers. However, the flip-side is the potential for revenue volatility, etc. (risks are below).

  • Revenue volatility (e.g., Large loss in Apple’s chipset business (see pie-chart))
  • Smartphone market becoming mature
  • Tariffs and trade war
  • IoT is an evolving market with new competitors

I believe that Qualcomm’s lost Apple business, of which will be 80% gone by 2027, is a medium-term headwind, but it’s not a “death-blow” as Qualcomm has known about this for two years, and it’s been forced to enter new markets. Frankly, I believe when we look back on this, we’ll view it as an opportunity. As Winston Churchill said, “Never let a good crises go to waste.”

Valuation

The Apple customer-loss, worries over an “AI indigestion” phase, and the Trade War have pushed down QCOM’s share price. The table below shows different EV/forward Revenue scenarios and what QCOM’s shares would be worth for each (blue bar). QCOM trades at 3.9x EV/Rev as of this writing. While it could conceptionally trade at 2.5x (similar to Intel), I believe that is a low probability unless we’re in a tighter monetary situation as was the case in 10/2022 (see chart).

Alternatively, it’s doubtful QCOM can trade at 7.8x Revenue (12/2020) or at 29.6x (ARM Holdings) as 2020-21 was a period of excess monetary liquidity and Qualcomm isn’t a pure-licensing company like ARM.

An EV/Revenue ratio of 6x seems achievable (even considering slowing growth). This translates to a stock price of $235/share, or 55% greater than today’s price.

Conclusion:

As many readers know, I follow the blockchain/technology space closely. We’re witnessing a movement from centralization & control (e.g., Amazon, Google) to decentralization – both in “Web2” and blockchain. It isn’t just control of our private-data, but processing power is moving from a centralized to decentralized one.

Even in the highly centralized hyperscaler space, we’re witnessing decentralization as AI models shift from training on horizontal LLM (Large Language Models) to vertical [SLM] models. Concurrently, processing is moving from training at huge data-centers to inference at the edge (smartphones, autos, robots, appliances, etc.)

When one considers how well-suited Qualcomm is to the Exponential Age, it becomes obvious that Qualcomm shares are a “Strong Buy.”

data.title: Exxon Mobil Corporation: Bullish Momentum Should Be Fueled Up
data.description:
Exxon Mobil Corporation (NYSE:XOM) maintains a decent topline performance amid energy commodity price volatility. It perfectly understands market dynamics and how it can balance volume and margins. Despite market uncertainties, XOM remains well-positioned with its prudent business portfolio diversification and strategic inventory management. In addition, its solid balance sheet shows it can cover its large operating capacity, borrowings, and dividends. These can support my bullish view of this stock, as it appears a bit underpriced.

XOM’s Q4 2024: Challenged But Solid

Exxon Mobil Corporation has faced various challenges in recent years amid global economic and political uncertainties. Yet, it continues to exude resilience and maintain a decent performance and strategic market positioning. These are reflected by its recent performances. Its operating revenue amounted to $83.4B, down by -1.1% YoY from $84.3B. Various aspects contributed to it.

First, oil price volatility remained a challenge for the company. As we can see, WTI Crude Oil price dropped from its peak price of $86-87 per barrel to $70-71 at the end of the year. Meanwhile, the price of Brent plunged from $91-92 to $74-76 per barrel. The increased output of non-OPEC producers like the US put downward pressure on prices. This mainly affected XOM’s upstream segment. Note that XOM derives a substantial portion of its revenues from its oil and gas production. As we can see, its crude oil and bitumen net production volume reached 3.2M barrels per day versus 2.6M in Q4 2023.

Another factor was the sticky inflation that further softened spending and consumption across various industries. We also saw a -64% monthly import drop in October as more products were rerouted to inventory restocking. The massive rebound in the US Dollar strength measure in DXY was also a factor, making US-based oil and gas more expensive for other countries. All these things contributed to a lower demand for its refined products, such as gasoline and diesel.

Despite this, natural gas experienced a sustained strength, which partially offset the weakness in its oil business. In addition, XOM maintained prudent crude oil inventory management and scaled back its exploration activities and production to stabilize costs and margins. As we can see, its net oil upstream volumes rose by 17% or over 400,000 barrels per day from Q4 2023 to Q2 2024. In my view, that was logical considering the increasing crude oil prices and stable DXY. But in the second half, its upstream volume increase was slowed to 7.6%.

This proved effective as its net income margin remained flat at 9.5%. In addition, it lowered its cost sensitivity as the operating leverage decreased from 76.3% to 74.4%. The lower variable cost contribution makes XOM more resilient to inflation.

Challenges

Amid its stable topline performance, XOM still faces some challenges, such as the following.

OPEC Output Hike To Affect Oil Prices… Some More

OPEC expressed its intent to raise its output in the next two months by 138,000 bpd and 135,000 bpd, respectively. This can influence global oil prices, which may prompt non-OPEC countries like the US to lower their prices. The impact will be evident in Brent and WTI. They initially rebounded by 13% in anticipation of President Trump’s inauguration last January. Yet, this was short-lived as global uncertainties persist.

Considering OPEC’s move, their prices may either stay within its current range or decrease further. Note that their prices remain near their 52-week lows. So, XOM may experience a weaker pricing flexibility to remain competitive.

On the flip side, its effect in the long run can still benefit the company. Lower oil prices can temper inflation, resume policy easing, and encourage consumption across industries. These can bolster economic activities and increase the demand for its oil and gas, as well as its refined products.

Strong DXY

The DXY is still in a solid upward momentum and considering the economic condition of the US relative to other countries, the trend is likely to continue this year. The Fed tempered inflation after temporarily pausing its rate cuts. Additionally, its GDP growth and unemployment rate are starting to normalize and return to pre-pandemic levels.

While XOM can anticipate an increased domestic demand, a strong DXY can still affect its sales in other countries. This will be more evident in those with weaker currencies against the USD.

Mitigants and Drivers

These are the things I believe can help XOM cushion those risks and even drive its rebound.

Strong Balance Sheet and Cash Flows

Despite its high capital intensity, XOM boasts impressive liquidity and sustainability with its strong cash flows. Its free cash flow or FCF reached $8.0B versus $7.97 in Q4 2023. This means that XOM generated higher cash returns despite a weaker market condition. So, XOM was able to convert nearly 10% of its revenues into free cash. If we exclude the impact of working capital, the value will be $9.5B.

Looking into its Balance Sheet, its cash, inventory, and debt levels remain relatively stable. This also proves how prudent business portfolio diversification and inventory management worked. By knowing what and how much to produce relative to the market, XOM managed its inventory very well. As a result, it avoided unnecessary cash burns and/or financial leverage.

Moreover, its Net Debt/EBITDA remains very low at 0.28x. This only shows that XOM has enough cash reserves and earnings to pay all its borrowings within a quarter of a year. In addition, 88% of its borrowings will not mature within this year. To be more specific, 34% of all borrowings have maturities of more than a decade. So, XOM has a lot of time to cover its borrowings, and a portion of its cash inflows can sustain dividends.

Natural Gas Demand Surge

I already talked a lot about its oil and haven’t even touched natural gas a bit. So, I am reserving this specific section for natural gas. I believe this will be one of the key drivers of XOM despite the increasing calls from government agencies for renewable energies.

Natural Gas is also Exxon Mobil’s cornerstone. Last Q4 2024, it produced 8,331 million cubic feet per day, a 9.0% YoY increase. That is warranted due to the upsurge in demand and price. Currently, it costs $3.98, more than twice its price a year ago. I believe the upsurge will continue considering the increasing demand from the power sector and LNG shipping capacity. Even the US EIA affirms this as it expects natural gas to stabilize at $3.80 before rebounding to $4.20, driven by the anticipated increase in LNG exports. Likewise, the International Energy Agency expects the demand-supply imbalance or shortage to drive natural gas prices further this year. One of the reasons is the increasing global gas demand by 2.8% or 115B cbm in 2024. Another reason is the geopolitical tension in Eastern Europe.

Natural Gas and Data Centers

I believe the demand surge for natural gas is not just a spur of the moment. Although natural gas is not a clean or renewable energy, it can be a transitional fuel source due to lower CO2 emissions.

What I believe will drive its demand and price is the global expansion of data centers. At this point, data centers use 3% of the global electricity supply. This is larger than the total consumption in the UK. This year, Deloitte anticipates the consumption to remain above 500 TWh.

Electricity consumption may increase further as billions or trillions of data center investments enter amid the AI peak. Amazon (AMZN) is a prime example as it intends to allot $100B investment in data centers amid the -ecommerce boom. In addition, the total count of data centers is expected to increase to 6,111 this 2025 and 8,378 in 2030.

In turn, data centers may require an additional 14 GW, equivalent to 2.22 Billion cubic feet per day or Bcf/d in the succeeding years. By 2050, the total demand for natural gas may increase to 24.1 Bcf/d. We must note that these numbers are solely for data centers.

As data centers increase electricity consumption, natural gas demand-supply equilibrium must be maintained. This can push its price up, leading to more XOM revenues. Also, these can have some spillovers on the oil business, since data centers may also require oil-powered generators to ensure continuity even if a grid failure takes place.

Price Ratio

XOM has increased dramatically in recent years. And I think that’s logical if we relate the stock price to its cash levels, expressed as cash per share. Currently, XOM trades at about 22x its cash. While it appears high, it remains cheap relative to the previous years with an average of 30x. If we use this Price/Cash Per Share Ratio to determine XOM’s fair value, the TP will be $160.45, a 34% upside potential.

To prove its logic, I also did a regression analysis. Their 79% correlation shows that cash per share has a substantial impact on price changes. Also, the p-value of 0.0064 confirms the reliability of their relationship. It means that only 0.64% of their direct correlation happened by chance, erasing the bias and randomness of the data. Although these are historical values, their high correlation can help us predict price trends, especially every time the company releases quarterly reports.

DCF Model

For a more forward-looking valuation, I also used the DCF Model. Like the P/CPS Ratio, the model also suggests that XOM is still cheap today. The TP of $140.88 suggests an 18% upside potential.

I assumed its perpetual growth rate at 2.00%. I considered the 2.0% target inflation of the Fed. Also, 2% is conservative and near the current inflation. After all, the performance of its oil segment is heavily impacted by macroeconomic indicators.

Meanwhile, I derived its WACC using the Capital Asset Pricing Model. I set its risk premium at 5% to cover the risk I will take should I invest here.

Technicals

XOM seems to be rebounding from its recent correction. As we can see, it just exceeded the 200-day SMA, showing a stronger momentum today. Also, the 50-day SMA confirms it as it starts to pick up momentum and comes closer to the 200-day SMA. This tells us that recent positive sentiments continue to push the price upward. Once the 50-day SMA exceeds the 200-day SMA, the bullish trend may continue.

MACD and RSI confirm our bullish view. In fact, early bullish reversal patterns were already detected last March 5. After the correction, the price consolidated before hitting a low of $105.44 and breached the previous low of $105.51. However, the stock’s momentum has already increased substantially, as shown by MACD and RSI. Their low points last March 5 were much higher than on December 20. True enough, the price rebounded. Currently, the price remains bullish. Although there are some bearish rejections, buying volumes still outpace them.

Lastly, note that buying volumes appear to be increasing, contrary to the decreasing selling volume. This tells us that more bulls are accepting the current price as a good entry point.

Conclusion

Exxon Mobil Corporation remains a leading figure in oil and natural gas exploration and production. There are visible headwinds today, but its operational resilience through strategic management and solid Balance Sheet can help it cushion those. Lots of unexpected market opportunities are coming to support its rebound and expansion. In addition, it remains underpriced with attractive upside potential and increasing momentum. I am giving this stock a strong buy rating.

data.title: What's Driving Investment Grade And Where Might It Be Headed?
data.description:
By Matthew Brill, Head of North America Investment Grade Credit | Todd Schomberg, Senior Portfolio Manager | Craig Altholz, Client Portfolio Manager

Investment grade bonds are up 2.34% this year, with much of its solid performance driven by a rally in intermediate yields.1 There has been a lot going on under the hood. Here’s our insights about the various market drivers year-to-date, and where we think investment grade is headed in the coming months.

Craig: Let’s start with rates since they have been the biggest driver of returns this year. They moved late last year, reflecting uncertainty over what the new Trump administration’s policies could mean for inflation. What’s your view on rates in the coming months?

Matt: The market started the year concerned about inflation driven by animal spirits (emotions) as the Trump administration was sworn in. We had a hot Consumer Price Index (CPI) print in January, but inflation has slowed, and the focus has shifted toward future growth. Markets are concerned about tariffs and their potential negative impact on the economy. There’s also concern around immigration policy, which could be growth-negative. Whether tariffs are inflationary, or just one-time price resets, is debatable, but the Federal Reserve (Fed) will likely need to navigate the price impacts of tariffs at the same time that the US economy may be slowing. Given a slower growth outlook, we’d expect the Fed to look through the inflationary impact of tariffs, allowing rates to go lower.

Craig: How have earnings been for industrials? Is credit expansion continuing to drive growth?

Matt: We've seen strong earnings from industrials this year. Utilities have had a somewhat more mixed performance. But if you look at traditional US industrials, they’ve done well.2 The outlook going forward may be more negative or mixed, however. As earnings reports have come out, a lot of companies have beat expectations, but guided forward expectations down, particularly consumer-related companies.

Corporate balance sheets are still in good shape overall. There has been a fair amount of issuance in the investment grade market and some in high yield, but it's been well received. Some companies are taking on debt to pay down debt coming due from previous years, but they appear to be aimed at not levering up.

Craig: Bonds have been negatively correlated to stocks in recent selloffs, such as January’s DeepSeek-related one. What did bonds do during the March stock correction?

Todd: Bonds performed as expected — they were inversely correlated to stocks in these risk-off events. Whenever we’ve had a selloff this year, interest rates have fallen and bonds have rallied. That’s exactly why they may be beneficial in a mix of assets, in our view, for that inverse correlation. With so much uncertainty, especially around growth, we think this negative correlation will continue throughout the rest of the year. Also, many investors have been yield buyers. Upward corrections in yields have the potential to draw buyers back into the market, so bonds could perform well in different situations.

Craig: Investment grade rating upgrades have outstripped downgrades, but Celanase and Nissan were downgraded to high yield. Do you think it’s symptomatic of broader trends or are they idiosyncratic? Also, are there any changes to your bull and bear cases for credit spreads this year?

Todd: The ratio of investment grade credit upgrades to downgrades has risen sharply since the 2020 COVID-19 crisis, to a record 4.7 in 2024.3 Celanese, a global chemical company, and the carmaker Nissan (OTCPK:NSANF)(OTCPK:NSANY) are two one-off situations, in our view, because the companies were poorly run and the downgrades weren’t related to broader fundamental concerns, such as tariffs.

At the end of the day, we still expect more upgrades than downgrades going forward. Of course, if tariffs cause economic activity to slow sharply in the second half of the year, fundamentals could be more challenged, but that isn’t our base case.

For spread expectations, our bull case (15%-20% probability) was for a 55 basis point spread on the investment grade index. That was predicated on a high all-in yield of 4.5% or more on the 10-year Treasury, but not exceeding 5%. In other words, our bull case was dependent on being in the sweet spot of the high 4% level in rates, but without the fear of blowing through the high end of the recent trading range, which might cause panic.

We believed that the high end of the range would likely attract yield-based buyers, such as insurance companies, annuities, and pension plans. Now that we’re trading in the low 4% range on the 10-year Treasury, we’re less optimistic about how much tighter spreads can go.

Our bear case was a widening to around a 120 basis points spread on the index, which has a very small probability, in our view. A key tail risk would be a serious policy misstep, for example. But though there is much volatility and noise around policy, we don't believe economic outcomes will be drastically negative.

Craig: We’ve had spirited debates about the consumer lately. It seems like there’s some softness at the lower end of the consumer spectrum, which has yet to show up in the macro numbers. Have you made any portfolio positioning shifts, or are you thinking about any new risks based on divergent consumer strength and behavior?

Matt: We recently did a deep dive on the consumer. The general conclusion was that there are consumer market segments that are getting squeezed, especially on the lower income end. Is the squeeze big enough to have an impact on the trajectory of the economy and potentially widen credit spreads? We don't think so. We expect some economic slowing this year, so we want to be cautious on certain areas of the economy that could be impacted, like subprime auto and credit card loans. The auto companies could also be susceptible, including foreign ones that could be hit by tariffs. So, we’ve been cautious on areas that could be impacted by a pullback from lower-end consumers. But we don't expect the current consumer dynamics to change the direction of gross domestic product (GDP) or the overall economy at this juncture.

Footnotes

  1. Source: Bloomberg US Credit Index. The index is up 2.34%, Jan. 1, 2025 to March 21, 2025.

  2. Source: According to FactSet, 78% of S&P 500 industrials companies reported earnings that were above market expectations in Q4 2024. Utilities also beat expectations in several cases, but only 48% reported beats versus consensus estimates, so the results were more mixed. FactSet, Earnings Insight as of March 20, 2025.

  3. Source: JPMorgan, JPM Daily Credit Strategy & CDS/CDX am update, Jan. 7, 2025.

Important information

NA4350644

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

A basis point is one-hundredth of a percentage point.

The Bloomberg US Credit Index measures the investment grade, US dollar-denominated, fixed-rate, taxable corporate, and government-related bond markets.

Fixed income investments are subject to credit risk of the issuer and the effects of changing interest rates. Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa. An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer’s credit rating.

Correlation is the degree to which two investments have historically moved in relation to each other.

The Consumer Price Index (CPI) measures the change in consumer prices and is a commonly cited measure of inflation.

A credit rating is an assessment provided by a nationally recognized statistical rating organization (NRSRO) of the creditworthiness of an issuer with respect to debt obligations, including specific securities, money market instruments, or other debts.

Credit risk is the risk of default on a debt that may arise from a borrower or issuer of bonds failing to make required payments.

Credit spread is the difference in yield between bonds of similar maturity but with different credit quality.

Gross domestic product (GDP) is a broad indicator of a region’s economic activity, measuring the monetary value of all the finished goods and services produced in that region over a specified period of time.

High yield bonds, or junk bonds, involve a greater risk of default or price changes due to changes in the issuer’s credit quality. The values of junk bonds fluctuate more than those of high quality bonds and can decline significantly over short time periods.

Idiosyncratic developments refer to unique events that do not affect an entire market or portfolio.

Inflation is the rate at which the general price level for goods and services is increasing.

Spread represents the difference between two values or asset returns.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of March 25, 2025. These comments should not be construed as recommendations but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties, and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.
